                                    8
Today's Topics
• Quick Review: Python Libraries

• Data Cleaning

• Data visualization
                                                                 9
Data Science Libraries in Python
• Python toolboxes/libraries
   ⎻ NumPy (manipulation of homogeneous array-based data)
   ⎻ Pandas (manipulation of heterogeneous and labeled data)
   ⎻ SciKit-Learn (machine learning)

• Visualization libraries
   ⎻ Matplotlib (publication-quality visualizations)
   ⎻ Seaborn (data visualization library based on matplotlib)
 Pandas vs NumPy                                                                       10



                Pandas                                       NumPy

Useful for tabular data of different types          Useful for numerical data

Can be indexed using keywords (similar to
                                                  Referenced by index number
            column names)

  Primary data types: Series (1D) and
                                                 Primary data type: Arrays (nD)
           DataFrame (2D)

       Consumes more memory                            Memory efficient

 Better performance when the number of       Better performance when number of rows
          rows is 500K or more                             is 50K or less

      Indexing series is very slow                 Indexing arrays is very fast
                                          11
Using Libraries in Python
# to use a library, you must import it


import pandas
pandas.read_csv("emp-with null.csv")
                                                     12
Renaming Libraries
# this...
import pandas
pandas.read_csv("emp-with null.csv")

# can be replaced with this...
# this is conventional and therefore preferred
import pandas as pd
pd.read_csv("emp-with null.csv")

                                             CODE
                                                 13
Assumption for Today's Lecture
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
emp = pd.read_csv("emp-with null.csv")




                                         CODE
                        14
Today's Topics
• Quick Review

• Data Cleaning

• Data visualization
 Data Integrity Problems                                           15



             Inconsistent Text Format




       Incorrect                        Missing Data   Outliers
       Data Type
 Missing Data Values                                        16


• Common issue in almost every real dataset
• Causes
   ⎻ Omission by respondents
   ⎻ Sensor failure
   ⎻ Problems merging data sets
• Problem
   ⎻ Can reduce (or break) performance of data analytics


• Missing values must be addressed!
    Missing Values                 17




                    In Excel:
                    Empty cells




In Pandas:
NaN
                                                                     18
       Different Flavours of Missing Values
 import numpy as np
 import pandas as pd
 df = pd.DataFrame(dict(age=[5, 6, np.NaN],
                             born=[pd.NaT,
                                      pd.Timestamp('1939-05-27'),
                                    pd.Timestamp('1940-04-25')],
                             name=['Alfred', 'Batman', ''],
                             toy=[None, 'Batmobile', 'Joker']))




▪ np.NaN: NumPy Not a Number
▪ pd.NaT: pandas Not a Time
▪ None: built-in Python object representing a null value
▪ '': empty string, common representation of missing or
  undefined values, especially in text
 Detect Missing Values using                            19

emp.isnull()




      emp.loc[35:45, 'years of experience'].isnull()
                                                                     20
       Different Flavours of Missing Values
 import numpy as np
 import pandas as pd
 df = pd.DataFrame(dict(age=[5, 6, np.NaN],
                             born=[pd.NaT,
                                      pd.Timestamp('1939-05-27'),
                                    pd.Timestamp('1940-04-25')],
                             name=['Alfred', 'Batman', ''],
                             toy=[None, 'Batmobile', 'Joker']))
 df.isnull()



▪ np.NaN: NumPy Not a Number
▪ pd.NaT: pandas Not a Time
▪ None: built-in Python object representing a null value
▪ '': empty string, common representation of missing or
  undefined values, especially in text
                                                          22
isnull().any()
emp.isnull().any() returns a list of column names that
have NaN values in them
                                                          23
Display Names of Columns with Null Values
# print(emp.columns)
columns_withNull = emp.columns[emp.isnull().any()]
columns_withNull

Result:


Display Names of Columns without Null Values
#print(emp.columns)
columns_withoutNull = emp.columns[emp.notnull().all()]
columns_withoutNull

Result:
 Display Data of the Columns with or                    25

without Null Values
• Display data in columns containing null values
     columns_withNull = \
            emp.columns[emp.isnull().any()]
     emp[columns_withNull]
     # columns selection using dictionary mechanism,
     # a list of column names in []


• Data in columns not containing null values
     columns_withoutNull = \
            emp.columns[emp.notnull().all()]
     emp[columns_withoutNull]
                                                          26
Axis
• A DataFrame object has two axes
   ⎻ “axis 0” represents rows
   ⎻ “axis 1” represents columns
• The DataFrame object to the right has
   ⎻ "axis 0": five rows indexed by 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
   ⎻ “axis 1”: two columns along indexed: ‘a’ and ‘b’.
                                            27
isnull().any(axis=…)
• Using isnull().any(axis=0) to identify
 all columns with missing values
     emp.isnull().any(axis=0)
     # equivalent to emp.isnull().any()



• Using isnull().any(axis=1) to identify
 all rows with missing values
     emp.isnull().any(axis=1)
                                                                    28
Display Records with Missing Values
• Display the rows that have missing values
  emp[emp.isnull().any(axis=1)] # Filtering using Boolean Series
                                                             29
Exercise
• Count the rows where 'years of experience' value is not
 missing:

  print(len(emp[emp['years of experience'].notnull()]))


  # alternatively
  emp['years of experience'].notnull().sum()
 Options for dealing with Missing                                30

Values
1. Drop records with missing values ("complete case"; used in
   a lot of scientific research)
2. Imputation: Use the most probable value to replace the
  missing values. Possibilities…
  1. a default (assumed) value
  2. mean/median (when data distribution is symmetric​)
  3. regression imputation
 Retain Only Complete Records                   31


• This is preferred in this course
   emp_withoutMissingValueAll = emp.dropna()
   print(len(emp_withoutMissingValueAll))
   emp_withoutMissingValueAll
   emp_withoutMissingValueAll.isnull().any()
 Filling Blanks with Assumptions (1/2)                        32


• Fill missing values with the most probable value
  # Use this strategy when we know the most probable value
  # E.g., here we fill in 0 if we know from domain experts
  # that 0 is the most probable value
  filled_emp1 = emp.fillna(0)
  filled_emp1.loc[30:39]
                                                           33
Filling Blanks with Assumptions (2/2)
• Fill missing values with the mean value
  filled_emp2 = emp.fillna(emp.mean(numeric_only=True))
  filled_emp2.loc[30:39]
 Dealing with Invalid or Nonsensical                                 34

Values
• Drop the rows with the data: you can use boolean
 indexing to remove the rows that contain the nonsensical
 data
• Replace the data with a default value: you can use the
 replace() method to replace the nonsensical data with
 a default value, such as NaN or “Unknown”
• Keep the invalid data as it is: if the data does not affect the
 overall analysis, you can choose to not take any action
                                                                         35
Aggregation Functions in Pandas
• Aggregation – computing a summary statistic about each group, i.e.,
   ⎻ compute group sums or means;
   ⎻ compute group sizes/counts, etc.

• Common aggregation functions:
   ⎻ min, max
   ⎻ count, sum, prod
   ⎻ mean, median, mode, mad
   ⎻ std, var
     agg() Method                                                                       36


Useful when multiple statistics are computed per column
filled_emp1[['years of experience','impact factor']].agg(['min','mean','max','sum'])




filled_emp2[['years of experience','impact factor']].agg(['min','mean','max','sum'])
 Summary of Methods to Deal With                                                   37

Missing Values
• There are a number of methods to identify and deal with
 missing values in a DataFrame

 DataFrame’s methods     Description
 dropna()                Drop rows with at least one null value
 dropna(thresh = 5)      Drop rows with less than 5 non-missing values, i.e.,
                         less than 5 valid values
 fillna(0)               Replace missing values with zeros
 isnull()                Return True if the value is missing
 notnull()               Return True for non-missing values, i.e., valid values
                                                                   38
Dealing with Duplicates
• In some cases, we need to deal with duplicates (e.g., tweets)
   emp[emp.duplicated()] #check for duplicate rows
• Pandas provides drop_duplicates() to deal with duplicates
   ⎻ keep only the first duplicate record (row)
   emp_noDup = emp.drop_duplicates(keep='first')
   ⎻ keep only the last duplicate record
   emp_noDup = emp.drop_duplicates(keep='last')
   ⎻ Remove all duplicate records
   emp_noDup = emp.drop_duplicates(keep=False)
 39
                        40
Today's Topics
• Quick review

• Data cleaning

• Data visualization
   Data Visualization (Plotting) Objectives            41

• Present information   • Communicate Deep Insights
                                                          42
Data Visualization Principles
• Charts should tell a story
   ⎻ Graphics should be clear on their own
   ⎻ Chart should yield insight beyond the text


• Charts should have graphical integrity
   ⎻ They shouldn’t “lie” (mislead the viewer)


• Charts should avoid unnecessary graphical complexity
   ⎻ The simpler the better
                                                                                      43
Why is Data Visualization Important?
• Amplifies your message
• Gives meaning to your data
• Saves time
• Makes for better decision making
• Is more shareable and digestible



                               The beauty of data visualization - David McCandless
                               https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5Zg-C8AAIGg
                                                                         44
Python Libraries for Visualisation

                        Seaborn

Matplotlib              • Built on top of matplotlib
                        • Targets statistical data visualization
• Used for plotting       specifically
• Highly customizable   • Provides high-level interface for drawing
• Works well with         attractive statistical graphics, similar to
  NumPy and Pandas        ggplot2 library in R
                        • Uses simpler syntax with good default
                          themes
                        • Works with Pandas DataFrame
  Common Chart Types                                           46

Line Plot              Histogram                  Bar Chart




            Box Plot               Scatter Plot
                                                       47
Line Plots


• Used for numeric data
• Used to show trends
• Compare two or more different variables over time
• Could be used to make predictions
                                                                   48
Generating Line Plots with Matplotlib (1/2)
  import matplotlib as mpl
  import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
  x_list = [1,2,3,4,5]
  y_list = [2,3,4,5,6]
  fig, plot_1 = plt.subplots() # Create a figure using subplot.
  plot_1.plot(x_list, y_list)


  Can you understand the plot?
  Captions? Style? x and y variables?
                                               49
Generating Line Plots with Matplotlib (2/2)

Add title, x axis label, and y axis label
 import matplotlib as mpl
 import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
 x_list = [1,2,3,4,5]
 y_list = [2,3,4,5,6]
 fig, plot_1 = plt.subplots()
 # Create a figure using subplot.
 plot_1.plot(x_list, y_list)

 # Plot some data
 plot_1.set_title('My First Plot')
 plot_1.set_xlabel('x label')
 plot_1.set_ylabel('y label')
                                                                50
Plot Two Lines
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
x_list = [1,2,3,4,5]
y_list = [2,3,4,5,6]
z_list = [6,5,4,3,7]
fig, plot_1 = plt.subplots()
# Create a figure using subplot.
# Plot some data on the axes.
plot_1.plot(x_list, y_list, color='orange', label='group y')
plot_1.plot(x_list, z_list, color='black', label='group z')
plot_1.set_title('Two groups', fontsize=20)
plot_1.set_xlabel('Independent Variable', fontsize=16)
plot_1.set_ylabel('Dependent Variable', fontsize=16)
plot_1.legend() # enable legend
plt.show()
                                           51
Histograms
• Used for continuous data
• Displays the frequency distribution
• Summarize large data sets graphically
• Compare multiple distributions
                                                   52
Histogram of Salary using Matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
#we will use the emp dataset and set bins to 10
plt.hist(emp['salary'], bins=10)
plt.grid(linestyle='--')
plt.title('Salary Histogram')
plt.xlabel('salary')
plt.ylabel('count')
plt.show()
                                           53
Styles
• There are different available
 Matplotlib styles that can instantly
 change the appearance of plots. We
 have been using the default style.


• We can easily switch to other styles.

• Display the available styles by
      plt.style.available
                                            54
Using a Style and Reset Later
• To set a style for the current session
      plt.style.use('ggplot')


• Reset to the default style with
      plt.style.use('default')
                                                                    55
Bar Chart
• Used for nominal or ordinal categories
• Compare data amongst different categories
• Ideal for more than 3 categories
• Often used to count the occurrence of an entity in categoric
 variable - each entity of the categoric variable is represented
 as a bar
 Generate Bar Charts using Series’                                        56

built-in method plot()
emp['position'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar', rot=0)
# .value_counts() returns a Series in this case
# kind -- specifies the type of plot to be created
# rot -- specifies the rotation angle of the x-axis labels in degrees.
#    Rot=0 (horizontal) is default.
                                                            57
Box Plots
• Often called aka box and whisker plots
• Statistical graph used on sets of numerical data
• Shows the range, spread, center and potential outliers
• Used to compare data from different categories
                          58
How to Read a Box Plot
 Using DataFrame’s built-in method                               59

boxplot()
emp.boxplot(by ='position', column =['salary'], grid = False)
# specify the variable by which the data will be grouped
                                                                     60
Bar Charts vs. Box Plots
• Bar charts and box plots are omnipresent in the scientific
  literature.
• They are typically used to visualize quantities associated with
  a set of items.
• Representing the data accurately, however, requires choosing
  the appropriate plot according to the nature of the data and
  the task at hand.
• Bar charts are more appropriate for counts, whereas box
  plots are often used to represent the characteristics of a
  distribution.
 Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.2807.pdf
                                                                    61
Scatter Plots
• Used to visualize a relationship between two numeric variables
• Used to visualize correlation in a large data set
• Predict behavior of dependent variable based on the measure of
 the independent variable
   Plot y (salary) versus x (years of                        62

  experience) using Matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, grid = plt.subplots()
# Create a figure using subplot
# Can create multiple plots in a single figure
x = emp['years of experience']
y = emp['salary']
grid.scatter(x, y, color='orange')
grid.set_xlabel('Years of Experience', fontsize=12)
grid.set_ylabel('Salary', fontsize=12)
grid.set_title('Salary vs. Work Experience', fontsize=14)
                                                                                  63
Interactive Widgets
• Graphical elements that allow users to interact with the program, such as
 buttons, sliders, dropdown menus, and text boxes, and dynamically
 change the behavior or output of a program.
• Widget Libraries
• Basic Widget Types
   ⎻ Buttons: Create buttons that perform actions when clicked.
   ⎻ Labels: Display text or images.
   ⎻ Text Boxes: Allow users to enter and edit text.
   ⎻ Dropdown Menus: Present a list of choices for users to select from.
   ⎻ Sliders: Allow users to select a value from a range by dragging a slider.
   ⎻ Checkboxes: Provide options for selecting multiple choices.
                                                                          64
Interactive Widgets
• Widget Interactions: widgets can interact with each other and
 respond to user actions, e.g., clicking a button can trigger an event
 or update the value of another widget.
• "ipywidgets" is a Python package that provides interactive widgets
 for the Jupyter notebook environment. These widgets allow you to
 create interactive and visual elements such as buttons, sliders,
 dropdowns, and more.

 Anaconda Prompt (Run as administrator)
 conda install -c conda-forge ipywidgets
 Import the library in Jupyter Notebook
 import ipywidgets as widgets
                                                                    65
Interactive Widgets
#Create a Slider
#we can define the minimum and maximum values, the interval size
(step), a description and an initial value:
widgets.IntSlider(
    min=0,
    max=10,
    step=1,
    description='Slider:',
    value=5
)
                                                                           66
Histogram of Salary using Seaborn
The colour and styling are excellent in Seaborn, compared to Matplotlib
   import seaborn as sns
   p = sns.histplot(emp['salary'],bins=10,kde=True)
   #kde stands for 'kernel density estimate’, a way to estimate the
   probability density function (PDF).
   plt.grid(linestyle='--')
   p.set(ylabel = "count", title= "Salary Histogram")
                                                 67
Bar Chart using Seaborn
   sns.set_style("whitegrid")
   ax = sns.countplot(x='position', data=emp)
   plt.grid(linestyle='--')
   ax.set(title= "Count by Position")
 Bar Chart with hue Parameter in                                  68

Seaborn
Draw a bar chart with a hue that determines the colour using a
grouping variable
     sns.set_style("whitegrid")
     ax = sns.countplot(x='position', hue='gender', data=emp)
     plt.grid(linestyle='--')
     ax.set(title= "Count by Position")

     #alternatively
     plt.title("Count by Position")
                                                    69
Box Plot using Seaborn
 sns.boxplot(x='position', y='salary', data=emp)
                                                                  70
Side-by-side Box Plot using Seaborn
 sns.boxplot(x='position', y='salary', data=emp, hue='gender')
 # does this look nice?
                                                                  71
Let’s Move the Legend
 sns.boxplot(x='position', y='salary', data=emp, hue='gender')
 plt.legend(loc='right') # does this look nice?
                                                                  72
Let’s Move the Legend Again
 sns.boxplot(x='position', y='salary', data=emp, hue='gender')
 plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.25, 0.5))
 # what do 1.25 and 0.5 mean?
                                                                  73
Scatter Plots using Seaborn
sns.scatterplot(data=emp, x="years of experience", y="salary")
plt.grid(linestyle='--')
 Regression Line on Top of a Scatter                                 74

Plot using Seaborn
sns.regplot(x='years of experience',
            y='salary',
            data=emp).set(title='Salary vs. years of experience')
                                                            75
Other Plots
• Heatmap
• Density Plot
• Joint Distribution Plot
•…

Refer to: https://seaborn.pydata.org/examples/index.html
             76
Questions
